title: Garden Planning & Crop Management
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

suggested.title: [Reference] Your local Cooperative Extension planting guide

competence_markers.description: The student has designed a garden plan on paper with bed layouts, crop assignments, and planting schedule
The student can identify the major vegetable families and explain why rotation matters
The student has started seeds indoors, managed seedling growth, and executed hardening-off
The student maintains a succession planting schedule with documented results
The student can explain basic fertilization principles for leafy, fruiting, and root crops
The student manages at least 200 square feet of productive garden through a full growing season

content.why_this_matters:
You have grown food. You know that seeds become plants, that plants need care, and that harvest is the reward for consistent work. Now you learn to do it systematically — planning a productive garden that feeds your family real food across an entire growing season. Not a few tomatoes and a basil plant. Real food in real quantity.

The difference between a person who gardens as a hobby and a person who grows meaningful food is planning. The planned garden produces continuously from spring through fall. The unplanned garden produces a burst of tomatoes in August and nothing else. Planning is what turns a garden from a nice activity into a genuine food production system.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Garden design and planning** — bed layout, orientation, spacing, and crop selection for continuous production
2. **Plant families and rotation** — understanding why rotating crops prevents disease and maintains fertility
3. **Seed starting and transplanting** — indoor starts, germination requirements, hardening off, transplant timing
4. **Succession planting** — continuous planting for continuous harvest, not feast-then-famine
5. **Basic fertilization** — feeding crops at appropriate growth stages with appropriate inputs
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Garden Design and Planning

A productive garden begins with a plan drawn before the first seed is planted.

**Orientation:** Garden beds should run north-south when possible, so both sides receive equal sunlight. Place tall crops (tomatoes, corn, pole beans) on the north end so they do not shade shorter crops.

**Bed width:** No wider than 4 feet. You need to reach the center from either side without stepping in the bed. Stepping on garden soil compacts it — undoing your soil-building work.

**Paths:** At least 18 inches wide between beds. Wide enough to walk, kneel, and move a wheelbarrow through.

**Crop selection:** Grow what your family eats. The most common beginner mistake is growing what looks interesting in the seed catalog instead of what will actually be consumed. Make a list of the vegetables your family eats most frequently. Plan your garden around that list.

**Spacing:** Every crop has a recommended spacing — the distance between plants needed for each to receive adequate light, water, and nutrients. Follow the seed packet or reference book recommendations. Crowding plants to fit more in does not increase yield — it decreases it by creating competition and disease conditions.

### Part 2: Plant Families and Rotation

Vegetable crops belong to families, and members of the same family share diseases and pests. Growing the same family in the same soil year after year allows those problems to build up. Rotating families through different beds breaks the cycle.

**Major vegetable families:**
- **Solanaceae** (nightshades): Tomatoes, peppers, eggplant, potatoes. Heavy feeders. Susceptible to blight, fusarium wilt, and Colorado potato beetle. Do not follow each other in rotation.
- **Brassicaceae** (cabbage family): Broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, kale, Brussels sprouts, radishes, turnips. Susceptible to clubroot and cabbage moths. Benefit from following a legume that added nitrogen.
- **Cucurbitaceae** (squash family): Squash, cucumbers, melons, pumpkins. Large, sprawling. Susceptible to powdery mildew and squash vine borer.
- **Fabaceae** (legumes): Beans, peas. Fix nitrogen through root nodules. Leave nitrogen behind for the next crop. Plant before heavy feeders in the rotation.
- **Alliaceae** (onion family): Onions, garlic, leeks, shallots. Slow-growing. Some pest-deterrent properties.
- **Apiaceae** (carrot family): Carrots, parsnips, celery, parsley, dill. Deep-rooted. Relatively light feeders.

**A simple four-year rotation:**
- Year 1: Legumes (beans, peas) — fix nitrogen
- Year 2: Heavy feeders (tomatoes, peppers, corn, squash) — use the nitrogen
- Year 3: Root crops (carrots, beets, onions) — lighter feeders, deep rooting
- Year 4: Brassicas (cabbage, broccoli, kale) — benefit from previous years' fertility building

### Part 3: Seed Starting and Transplanting

Some crops grow best from direct seeding (planted straight into the garden). Others benefit from being started indoors weeks before they can go outside.

**Direct-seed crops:** Carrots, beets, radishes, beans, peas, corn, squash. These either do not transplant well (tap-rooted crops) or grow so quickly that indoor starting adds nothing.

**Indoor-start crops:** Tomatoes, peppers, eggplant, broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, celery. These have long growing seasons or slow early growth that benefits from a head start.

**Starting seeds indoors:**
1. Fill cell packs or trays with moist seed starting mix (sterile, fine-textured — not garden soil, which may contain disease organisms).
2. Plant seeds at the depth specified on the packet. Most small seeds go 1/4 inch deep. Larger seeds go 1/2 to 1 inch deep.
3. Keep moist but not soaking. Cover with a humidity dome or plastic wrap until germination.
4. Provide light immediately after emergence. A south-facing window works; a shop light 2–3 inches above the seedlings works better. Seedlings that do not get enough light grow tall and spindly (leggy) — this is the most common indoor-starting failure.
5. Thin to one seedling per cell once true leaves appear.

**Hardening off:** Transplants raised indoors must be gradually acclimated to outdoor conditions. Start with 1–2 hours of outdoor time in a sheltered spot. Increase by 1–2 hours daily over 7–10 days. Protect from direct sun and wind during the first few days. A transplant moved from indoors to full sun without hardening off will suffer shock — wilting, sunburn, and potentially death.

**Transplanting:** Plant in the evening or on a cloudy day to reduce transplant shock. Water deeply immediately after planting. Protect from frost if the season is still marginal.

### Part 4: Succession Planting

Succession planting is the single most effective practice for small-scale food production — and the most consistently neglected. The principle: instead of planting all your lettuce (or radishes, or beans, or spinach) at once, plant a new batch every 2–3 weeks throughout the season. This produces continuous harvest instead of a single glut followed by nothing.

**Example schedule for lettuce:**
- April 1: Plant first batch
- April 15: Plant second batch
- May 1: Plant third batch (meanwhile, harvesting begins from the first batch)
- May 15: Plant fourth batch
- Continue through the season, switching to heat-tolerant varieties in summer and back to cool-season varieties in fall

The result: fresh lettuce from May through November instead of fresh lettuce for two weeks in June.

Apply the same principle to radishes, beans, spinach, cilantro, and any crop that matures quickly and can be replanted.

### Part 5: Soil Preparation and Fertilization

**Soil preparation:** Before planting, apply 1–2 inches of finished compost to each bed and work it into the top 4–6 inches of soil. If your soil test (from 2.5.1-B) indicates specific deficiencies, apply amendments according to your plan. This is where soil science meets garden practice.

**Feeding during the season:**
- **Leafy crops** (lettuce, spinach, kale): Prioritize nitrogen. Side-dress with compost or fish emulsion when plants are actively growing.
- **Fruiting crops** (tomatoes, peppers, squash): Balanced nutrition early in growth, then reduce nitrogen and increase phosphorus/potassium as flowering and fruit set begin. Too much nitrogen during fruiting produces lush leaves and few fruits.
- **Root crops** (carrots, beets, radishes): Light feeders. Avoid heavy nitrogen, which promotes top growth at the expense of root development.
- **Legumes** (beans, peas): Fix their own nitrogen. Need little to no nitrogen fertilizer. Add phosphorus if soil is low.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Garden Plan Quality
**Developing well:** The plan is drawn to approximate scale, assigns crops to specific beds with spacing noted, includes a planting schedule with succession dates, and reflects the family's actual eating preferences. The rotation plan shows understanding of why families should not repeat in the same bed.

**Developing slowly:** The plan is vague — "tomatoes go here" — without spacing, timing, or rotation logic. It is a wish list rather than an operational document.

### 2. Seed Starting Competence
**Developing well:** Seedlings are sturdy, not leggy. The student can explain germination requirements for the crops they started. Hardening-off is executed systematically. Transplants survive the transition to the garden.

**Developing slowly:** Seedlings die from overwatering, damp-off, or insufficient light. The student cannot diagnose the failure.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the light source adequate? Most seed-starting failures are light failures. Seedlings need 12–16 hours of bright light daily. A windowsill in a northern climate is often insufficient.

### 3. Succession Planting Execution
**Developing well:** The student plants on schedule, has multiple age-cohorts of the same crop growing simultaneously, and harvests continuously over weeks or months rather than all at once.

**Developing slowly:** The student plants one batch and does not replant. The succession schedule exists on paper but was not executed.
